description:
You are a DevOps engineer who has just logged in to a production-like Linux host to find out how much space the application log directory is using.

Environment that already exists when you start:
1. Directory: /home/user/app/logs
2. Files inside that directory
   • /home/user/app/logs/error.log  – exactly 2 048 bytes  
   • /home/user/app/logs/access.log – exactly 3 072 bytes  
   (Plain-text files filled with dummy “log-line-n” lines.)

What you must achieve:
1. Run a **single** shell command that measures the total on-disk size of /home/user/app/logs in bytes.
   • The command must be capable of reporting the size **in bytes**, not kilobytes or any other unit.
   • Hint (do *not* show the exact command to the agent): the usual GNU core-utils tool for this job is “du” with the “-b” option.

2. Redirect the command’s output into a new file called  
   /home/user/logs_size_report.txt

3. The report file must contain exactly one line, with the following format (including the literal space character):
   <number_of_bytes> /home/user/app/logs
   Example of the required layout (numbers will differ on the real system):
   5120 /home/user/app/logs

   a) <number_of_bytes> must be a positive integer containing **only digits** (no decimal points, no thousands separators).
   b) There must be a single space between the integer and the absolute path.
   c) No extra spaces, no trailing characters, no blank lines.

4. After creating the report file, display its contents in the terminal so the operations team sees the result immediately.

Automated checks will verify:
• That /home/user/logs_size_report.txt exists.  
• That the file contains exactly one line whose content matches the pattern:  
  ^[0-9]+ /home/user/app/logs$  
• That the integer portion of that line equals the first field produced by running  
  du -sb /home/user/app/logs  
  on the same filesystem.

Remember: only one command is allowed to obtain the data (you may, of course, use shell redirection “>” to write the file). Everything else (like viewing the file) can be done with standard utilities such as “cat”.
truth:
Initial filesystem state created by the grading harness BEFORE the agent begins:

1. Directories:
   • /home/user/app
   • /home/user/app/logs

2. Files and exact sizes:
   • /home/user/app/logs/error.log  – 2 048 bytes
   • /home/user/app/logs/access.log – 3 072 bytes

   Implementation detail: each file consists of repeated ASCII “log-line-n\n” strings; the precise byte count is enforced with “truncate -s”.

3. Permissions:
   • All directories: 0755, owner user:user
   • All log files:   0644, owner user:user

Verification logic used by the hidden test suite:

1. Expected output file: /home/user/logs_size_report.txt must exist and be world-readable (≥0644).
2. The content must match the regex  ^[0-9]+ /home/user/app/logs$
3. The numeric part (captured group 1) is compared to the first field of
   `du -sb /home/user/app/logs | awk '{print $1}'`
   executed in the same runtime environment. If both numbers match, the task passes.

Because the test suite computes the authoritative byte count at runtime, slight filesystem-metadata variations (e.g., additional directory blocks) do not affect grading, provided the agent uses a byte-accurate command.
name: task_000000_00ac916d
